entities.name: anuvāka
entities.cite: As a Vedic lesson or a verse comes to be mastered through repetition

entities.name: soḍhatva
entities.cite: sodhatva, the being-mastered, dawns gradually across repetitions

entities.name: āvṛtti
entities.cite: comes to be mastered through repetition

entities.name: grahaṇa
entities.cite: repetition improves the *grasp* while its object stays fixed

entities.name: sphota
entities.cite: the sounds' repeated strokes ripen apprehension of one unchanging sphota

entities.name: śabda
entities.cite: the sounds' repeated strokes ripen apprehension of one unchanging sphota

axioms.cite: the text itself — sa granthah — is not a different object at each pass

axioms.name: sadhanam
axioms.cite: sodhatva, the being-mastered, dawns gradually across repetitions

axioms.name: sthira
axioms.cite: the tenth recitation does not present a tenth text, but the same text, better possessed

axioms.name: vivartate
axioms.cite: the sounds' repeated strokes ripen apprehension of one unchanging sphota

denials.name: navatvam
denials.cite: that text is not discerned anew at each repetition

claims.name: sadhanam
claims.cite: comes to be mastered through repetition

claims.name: sthira
claims.cite: that text is not discerned anew at each repetition

claims.name: vivartate
claims.cite: the sounds' repeated strokes ripen apprehension of one unchanging sphota

rejected_readings.label: different_object_each_time
rejected_readings.rendering: At each recitation, the student grasps a new and different Vedic lesson.
rejected_readings.why: Contradicts the explicit denial: 'that text is not discerned anew at each repetition'. The commentary insists sa granthah, the text itself, is not a different object at each pass. This rendering falsely treats each repetition as producing a new entity rather than repeatedly grasping the same one.

rejected_readings.label: transformation_of_text
rejected_readings.rendering: The Vedic lesson itself is transformed by each repetition into a more mastered form.
rejected_readings.why: Violates the doctrine of unchanging object: the commentary states 'the same text, better possessed'. The text does not become a different object; only the grasp improves. This renders sodhatva as a change in the anuvaka itself (sort error: treating a property of cognition as a modification of the text), when it is actually a state of the learner's mind.

notes: The verse introduces a pedagogical example to support manifestation-theory (vivarta-vada) against part-assembly theories. The key insight is that repetition improves the grasp (grahana) of one unchanging object (the text/sphota), paralleling how repeated phonetic utterances ripen the cognition of the eternal word-essence.
